sections.html:
Taste is intellectual capital. You spent a decade accumulating it - pattern recognition, quality thresholds, decision frameworks - and right now you're letting it sit in a zero-yield account. You ask AI to draft a product strategy memo and it comes back sounding like a consultant who's never had a conviction in his life. Structurally competent. Generically correct. Missing every opinion that makes your thinking worth paying for.

Here's the thing. The AI isn't broken - it's uninformed. It has zero access to the decades of compounded judgment that make your work yours. That's not a prompt problem. That's a capital deployment problem. And the fix isn't handing your judgment to AI - it's empowering AI to work as an extension of your judgment. You, your knowledge store, and AI working together. That's the triad. That's where the value lives.

Your taste isn't aesthetics. It's intellectual capital across every domain where you hold earned opinions - strategy, hiring, product direction, brand voice, pricing, positioning. None of it reaches your AI right now. You are sitting on a pile of undeployed assets. That is insanity.

Put a dollar value on it. Your judgment on a single pricing decision might be worth $500k over 3 years. Your hiring instinct saves $200k every time it prevents a bad senior hire. Each judgment is a high-IRR asset you deploy maybe a dozen times a year. Deploy it 200 times a year through AI and the math gets absurd fast. The cost of unlocking it? A 90-minute writing session. There has never been an ROI curve this steep in any professional's career.

Imagine if every draft you got back already sounded like you. Your framing, your priorities, your specific way of cutting through noise. The 45 minutes you spend making AI output sound like something you'd actually send - gone. What's stopping you from making that real this week?

sections.title: Write Your First Taste Doc in 20 Minutes
sections.triggerText: See how to build a taste document
sections.html:
The Format

Open a new doc. Title it "[Your Domain] Taste Rules." Set a timer for 20 minutes. Write the answer to: "If I hired a brilliant junior who'd never worked in my domain, what are the 10 rules I'd give them on day one?"

Be Ruthlessly Specific

Not "write clearly" but "every brief starts with the user's current workaround, not the feature description." Not "I prefer quality" but "we kill features that don't move retention within 2 sprints."

Examples by Domain

  • Strategy: "We always frame market opportunities as customer pain points first, TAM second. We never pursue a segment without 5+ customer conversations."
  • Design: "Body text never exceeds 18px. Primary CTAs use high-contrast fills, never outlines. Section padding is 80px minimum on desktop."
  • Engineering: "Services are named by what they do, not what they contain. Error messages tell the user what to do next, not what went wrong."

Deploy It

Upload to a Claude Project or add it to your CLAUDE.md file. Test immediately by asking Claude to produce something in your domain. The difference will be obvious on the first try.

Think about it like this. A founder who's seen 200 pitch meetings has a model in her head that no MBA program teaches. A designer who's run 500 user tests sees flows the way a chess grandmaster sees boards. That model is the asset. The question isn't whether you have it - you do. The question is whether you've ever taken 20 minutes to write it down.

A product manager financialized her brief-writing craft this way. AI briefs now match her quality bar on the first draft. The 45-minute rewrite tax? Gone. But the real win isn't the $75 per interaction saved - it's what she does with those 45 minutes. Deep product thinking. Pressure-testing strategy. Designing experiments worth running. Time for the hard work nobody else has time for. That's not just efficiency. That's a fundamentally different job.

An engineering lead documented state management, API conventions, and component standards. The "fix it to match our style" step disappeared entirely. Zero rework cycles. A hiring manager encoded the 12 signals she uses to screen senior candidates - her time-to-hire dropped by 3 weeks and first-year retention jumped 40%. She used to say "I'll know it when I see it." Now her AI knows it too.

sections.title: Build a Shared Taste Layer for Your Team
sections.triggerText: Learn the framework for scaling taste across teams
sections.html:
The Three-Layer Capital Stack

Layer 1 - Company taste: Brand, strategy, quality standards that apply to everything. The foundation.

Layer 2 - Team taste: How product evaluates opportunities, how engineering defines "done," how design makes decisions. The execution layer.

Layer 3 - Individual taste: Personal judgment that layers on top. The PM who's strong on pricing. The designer with refined opinions about information architecture. The alpha layer.

How to Set It Up

Create a Notion database called "Taste Docs" with columns for Domain, Owner, and Last Updated. Have each senior person write their taste doc - 10-15 specific rules. Connect the Notion workspace to Claude via MCP. Every team member's AI now draws from the full taste stack.

The Result

A new PM's first brief matches the org's quality bar because the context carries it automatically. Taste scales without bottlenecks. No more "run it by the founder first" - the founder's judgment is in the system.

The punchline is compounding. Every rule you refine, every judgment call you record, every "here's how I think about this" you document - these compound across every future interaction. Your taste doc becomes a living asset that appreciates with use. But the asset isn't the doc alone - it's the triad: you continuing to exercise judgment, the knowledge store carrying your accumulated standards, and AI extending your capacity. You're not outsourcing your taste. You're deploying it through agents that build with you.

Picture this. A new hire joins on Monday. By Wednesday, her first output sounds like it was written by someone who's been at the company three years. Not because she's a prodigy, but because the AI she's working with carries every quality bar your senior people spent years developing. That's not onboarding - that's teleportation.

After 6 months, your AI carries judgment that would take a new hire a year to absorb - because you built it, you refine it, and you direct it. That's not a productivity hack. That's a moat.

sections.title: Real Examples of Taste in Production
sections.triggerText: See how companies are encoding taste right now
sections.html:
Airbnb

Their design team maintains a living document of design principles so specific that AI tools can reference them: illustrations use a warm, hand-drawn quality; photography shows real spaces with natural light; UI copy is conversational, never corporate.

Basecamp / 37signals

Decades of opinionated product philosophy documented publicly - "build less," "charge from day one." When their team uses AI, those principles aren't vague values. They're specific, deployable judgment calls.

Notion

Their internal writing guide specifies voice characteristics, sentence structures to avoid, and how to frame different content types. AI-assisted content starts from that taste layer - which is why their communications feel consistent across hundreds of team members.

Duolingo

Character voice guidelines encoded so precisely that AI-generated lesson content matches the tone their content team took years to develop. New writers' AI-assisted drafts sound like Duolingo from day one.

Taste documentation turns a knowledge base from a reference library into an opinionated decision engine. Raw knowledge is facts and data - necessary but commodity. Taste is your interpretation of what those facts mean and how they should influence action. Knowledge provides context. Taste provides judgment. Together, they produce AI output that's not just informed but opinionated - in the way that makes your work defensible and your position irreplaceable.

Knowledge without taste is a Wikipedia article. Taste without knowledge is guessing. Either without you directing AI is noise at scale. The builder who deploys all three - their ongoing judgment, their knowledge store, and AI as an extension of their craft - creates something no competitor can replicate. You're not outsourcing your differentiators. You're compounding them. That's the whole game.

Your strategic judgment - how you evaluate markets, size bets, decide when to pivot versus persevere - is the thing investors are actually funding. And right now, none of it reaches your AI. You ask for a board deck and get consultant-speak that could belong to any startup. The conviction that makes your thinking worth backing? Missing entirely. You spend 2 hours rewriting every output just to sound like yourself. That's a tax you're paying on undocumented judgment.

Here's the thing. That's not an AI capability problem. It's a capital deployment problem. Your taste in strategy, hiring, pricing, and positioning is intellectual capital sitting in a zero-yield account. Put a dollar value on it: a single pricing decision shaped by your judgment might be worth $500k over 3 years. A hiring call saves $200k every time it blocks a bad senior. These are high-IRR assets you currently deploy maybe a dozen times a year. Encode them once and deploy them 200 times a year through AI - the math gets absurd fast.

Imagine if every investor update, every board memo, every strategic brief already sounded like you wrote it. The 3 hours you burn rewriting drop to 20 minutes of directing judgment you already have. What's stopping you from doing that this week?

sections.title: Encode Your Strategic Judgment
sections.triggerText: See how founders are encoding strategic taste
sections.html:
Your Strategy Taste Doc

Write 10-15 rules that capture how you actually make decisions. Not platitudes - specifics: "We always frame market opportunities as customer pain points first, TAM second. We never pursue a segment without 5+ customer conversations. Competitive positioning leads with differentiation, not feature comparison."

Your Fundraising Judgment

Encode investor preferences, objection patterns, how you frame challenges as opportunities without sugarcoating, and the metrics you always lead with. One founder encoded this from 200+ pitch meetings - investor updates now take 20 minutes instead of 3 hours, more personalized than before.

Deploy It

Upload to a Claude Project. Test by asking for a board memo or competitive analysis. If it sounds like your thinking - your framing, your opinions, your quality bar - you've encoded enough.

AI now drafts board materials that reflect your actual strategic thinking. Not consultant-speak. Your thinking. The ROI on that documentation session is infinite because it compounds across every board deck, every investor update, every strategic memo for the life of the company.

Think about it like this. A founder who encoded his fundraising judgment saw it pay off six months later when a junior associate on his team wrote an investor memo using the same AI stack. For the first time in his company's history, he wasn't the last human in the chain for investor communications. That single change freed up 4 hours a week - time he redeployed into product conversations he'd been postponing for a year. The taste doc didn't just save his time. It redistributed it into the highest-leverage work.

But the real unlock is time. The hours you reclaim from rewriting go to the deep strategic work that actually differentiates - sitting with market shifts, pressure-testing positioning, designing the company's next act. Picture this: the strategic depth your company needs from you, finally happening.

sections.title: Scale Your Taste Across the Company
sections.triggerText: Learn how to remove yourself as the quality bottleneck
sections.html:
The Founder Bottleneck Problem

Your CEO judgment lives in your head. Your quality bar arrives as feedback too late to incorporate. Your team can't ship without your review because your taste is the only quality gate. That's not leadership. That's a single point of failure.

The Three-Layer Solution

Company taste: Brand, strategy, quality standards. Your non-negotiables encoded once.
Team taste: How product evaluates opportunities, how engineering defines "done," how design approaches users.
Individual taste: The PM who's strong on pricing, the engineer who's the expert on performance.

The Result

No more "run it by the founder first." Your strategic judgment is in the system. The team produces consistent work at 3x the speed. And your calendar? Finally clear for the strategic depth your company actually needs from you.

When a startup encodes only one domain of taste, they get consistency there and chaos everywhere else. When they encode strategy, product, hiring, voice, and design - every function produces work that carries organizational judgment. That's the difference between a team that uses AI and one that compounds through AI.

Think about the onboarding economics. Traditional ramp: 3-6 months before a new hire is fully productive, a senior leader burning 20% of their week mentoring, and quality drift on everything the newcomer touches. Annualized cost: $80-150k per hire. A shared taste layer compresses ramp from months to weeks, eliminates the senior mentor drag, and holds the quality bar from day one. You just turned a recurring cost center into a fixed-cost asset. Competitors who skip this will operate on a cost structure you can't be beaten on. What's stopping you from running a "taste week" at your company starting Monday? 90 minutes per leader is the entire ask.

sections.title: Taste Tools for Founders
sections.triggerText: See the tools founders are deploying
sections.html:
Claude Projects

Load your strategy docs, fundraising context, and competitive positioning as Project Knowledge. Every conversation draws from your judgment. Test it by asking for something in your domain - the difference is immediate.

Notion + MCP for Team Taste

Create a Notion database of taste docs. Connect via MCP. Every team member's AI draws from the full stack - company taste, team taste, individual taste - simultaneously.

The Compounding Advantage

After 6 months, your AI carries judgment a new VP would need a year to absorb. After 12 months, that's a competitive moat. Competitors can buy the same models. They cannot buy your compounded strategic judgment and the team culture built around it.

Specificity is the moat. Strategy, hiring, product, pricing, positioning - every domain where your founder judgment matters. The builder who deploys all three - their ongoing judgment, their knowledge store, and AI as an extension of their craft - creates something no competitor can replicate. Your competitors can buy the same models. They cannot buy the compounded strategic judgment you've been accumulating since the first customer call. That's the whole game.

Your product sense took years to build. The instinct that separates a retention-moving feature from a vanity metric. The quality threshold that knows when a brief is done versus when it's just long enough. The pattern recognition from 500 user interviews that sees through a customer's first answer to what they actually need. You ask AI for a product strategy and get a structurally competent document with zero conviction.

Here's the thing. The AI isn't bad at product - it's uninformed about YOUR product thinking. Your prioritization framework, your evidence bar, your opinion about what "done" means - none of it is accessible. That 45-minute rewrite you do on every AI draft? That's the tax on undocumented taste. Do the math: 45 minutes times every brief, every spec, every memo - that's $75 per interaction, easy. Run that across 200 interactions a year and you're burning a week of senior product thinking just to translate generic output into your reality.

Imagine if every feature brief came back already carrying your framing, your success metrics, your opinion about trade-offs. The 45 minutes you spend rewriting - gone. What's stopping you from putting your product brain into the system this week?

sections.title: Write Your Product Taste Doc
sections.triggerText: See how to encode your product judgment
sections.html:
Your Product Rules

Write 10-15 rules that capture how you actually make product decisions: "Every feature brief starts with the user's current workaround. Success metrics are behavioral, not engagement-based. We kill features that don't move retention within 2 sprints."

Your Prioritization Taste

Document how you weigh customer pain against business value, your threshold for "enough evidence," and how you frame trade-offs for engineering. One PM encoded this and sprint planning briefs now start 80% of the way to final. That's 4 hours reclaimed per sprint, every sprint, forever.

Deploy Immediately

Upload to a Claude Project along with your 3 best briefs as examples. Test it by asking for a feature brief in your domain. If the framing matches your thinking, you're there.

One product manager financialized her brief-writing craft. AI briefs now match her quality bar on the first draft. The 45-minute rewrite tax? Gone. But the real win is what she does with those 45 minutes - sitting with customer problems, pressure-testing strategy, designing experiments worth running. That's a fundamentally different job.

Think about it like this. A poker player who's played 10,000 hands has tells nobody taught her. A PM who's shipped 50 features has a model of trade-offs no framework captures. The question isn't whether you have it - you do. The question is whether you've taken 20 minutes to write it down. A product designer wrote 40 specific principles spanning persona-driven content hierarchy, UX flow logic, and visual taste. AI-generated concepts matched her product thinking without a single correction. First try. Like a poker player who finally wrote down her tells - now the AI plays her hand.

sections.title: Build Team Product Taste Infrastructure
sections.triggerText: Learn how to scale product quality without bottlenecks
sections.html:
The Problem

Your VP Product's quality bar arrives as feedback too late to incorporate. The PM's "done" and the designer's "done" measure against different bars. Quality only exists in the head of whoever reviews it.

The Three Layers

Company: Strategic frameworks, quality standards that apply across all products.
Team: How product evaluates opportunities, how you define success metrics, how you structure problem statements.
Individual: The PM who's strong on pricing. The researcher with the best user interview instincts. Personal alpha that layers on top.

Deploy via Notion

Create a Notion database of taste docs by domain. Connect to Claude via MCP. A new PM's first AI-assisted brief matches the team's quality bar because the context carries the org's collective product judgment automatically.

Product taste at scale means consistency without bottlenecks. The founder doesn't need to review every brief. The head of product doesn't need to enforce the evidence bar manually. The quality bar is in the system. Picture this: a new PM joins on Monday. By Wednesday, her first brief sounds like it was written by someone who's been at the company three years. Not because she's a prodigy, but because the AI she's working with carries every quality bar your senior PMs spent years developing.

And the leaders whose calendars cleared? They invest in the deep product thinking that differentiates - customer research, strategy, the hard product problems that have been waiting because nobody had time. The speed unlocks time. The time unlocks depth. That's derisking quality at scale while simultaneously raising the ceiling on what your best people can accomplish. A 3x speed improvement isn't just faster - it's a different kind of company.

sections.title: Product Taste in the Wild
sections.triggerText: See how product teams are encoding taste
sections.html:
Basecamp / 37signals

Decades of opinionated product philosophy documented publicly. "Build less. Charge from day one. Meetings are toxic." These aren't vague values - they're specific judgment calls that shape every AI-assisted output.

Figma

Top product teams encode not just visual style but product thinking - "progressive disclosure over feature density," "information architecture mirrors the user's mental model, not the database schema."

Custom Claude Projects

Thousands of PMs have built AI configurations loaded with their product taste docs. Prioritization frameworks, positioning rules, evidence thresholds. The pattern is consistent: document specificity, connect it, and the rewrite tax disappears.

Knowledge without taste is a Wikipedia article. Taste without knowledge is guessing. The product builder who deploys both - their ongoing judgment, their knowledge store, and AI as an extension of their craft - produces work that's not just informed but opinionated. That's what makes your product thinking defensible and your position irreplaceable. Every rule you refine compounds across every future interaction. After 6 months, your AI carries judgment that would take a new PM a year to absorb. That's not a productivity hack. That's a moat.

Your design taste took years to build. The eye that catches a 2px misalignment in a data table. The IA instinct that knows a nav problem is actually a mental-model problem. The visual judgment that separates refined from generic at a glance. And the product sense layered on top: which flows move activation, which features are vanity, and which interaction pattern will survive first-week drop-off. You ask AI for a screen concept and get a competent, characterless layout with zero conviction about the user it's for.

Here's the thing. The AI isn't bad at UI - it's uninformed about YOUR taste. Your spacing scale, your type hierarchy, your accessibility bar, your persona priorities, your opinion about what "done" means. None of it is accessible. That 30-minute cleanup pass you run on every AI draft? That's the tax on undocumented taste. Do the math: 30 minutes times every screen, every component variant, every empty state - run that across 200 artifacts a year and you're burning weeks of senior design thinking just translating generic output into your system.

Imagine if every concept came back already carrying your type scale, your token palette, your persona framing, and your activation metric. The 30 minutes of cleanup? Gone. What's stopping you from putting your design brain into the system this week?

sections.title: Write Your Product Taste Doc
sections.triggerText: See how to encode your design and product judgment
sections.html:
Your Design Rules

Write 15-20 specific rules that capture how you actually decide: "Primary CTAs are filled, never outlined. Body copy never exceeds 18px on desktop. Section padding is 80px minimum. Empty states always teach the next action. Progressive disclosure over feature density. IA mirrors the user's mental model, never the database schema. WCAG 2.2 AA is a floor, not a goal."

Your Product Judgment

Document how you weigh persona priority against business value, your threshold for "enough research," and how you tie each major flow to a north-star metric. One product designer encoded this and her screen concepts started shipping 80% of the way to final. That's hours reclaimed per week, compounding across every artifact she touches.

Deploy Immediately

Upload to a Claude Project along with your three strongest specs, your Figma token file, and your top personas. Test it by asking for a new onboarding flow in your domain. If the framing, hierarchy, and interaction choices match your thinking, you're there.

One product designer financialized her craft this way. AI concepts now match her quality bar and product framing on the first draft. The 30-minute cleanup tax? Gone. But the real win is what she does with those 30 minutes - deeper journey mapping, richer research synthesis, the tough IA problems that actually move retention. That's a fundamentally different job.

Think about it like this. A master typographer who's set 10,000 pages has micro-judgments no textbook captures. A product designer who's shipped 50 flows has a model of user intent no framework explains. The question isn't whether you have it - you do. The question is whether you've taken an afternoon to write it down. One designer wrote 40 specific principles spanning persona-driven content hierarchy, UX flow logic, progressive disclosure patterns, and visual taste. AI-generated concepts matched her thinking without a single correction. First try. Like a typographer who finally wrote down her micro-rules - now the AI sets the page in her voice.

sections.title: Build Team Product Taste Infrastructure
sections.triggerText: Learn how to scale design quality without bottlenecks
sections.html:
The Problem

Your design lead's critique arrives as Figma comments three days after the PR merged. The designer's "done" and the engineer's "done" measure against different bars. Quality only exists in the head of whoever reviews it. Design system governance is a Notion doc nobody opens.

The Three Layers

Company: Brand principles, accessibility baseline, and the north-star metrics every product is accountable to.
Team: How design evaluates opportunities, how the system is governed, how critique is run, how specs are structured.
Individual: The designer who's strong on IA. The one with the sharpest interaction instincts. The one who knows every empty state edge case. Personal alpha that layers on top.

Deploy via Notion

Create a Notion database of taste docs, principles, and system governance by domain. Connect to Claude via MCP and wire in your Figma library. A new designer's first AI-assisted concept matches the team's quality bar because the context carries the org's collective craft automatically.

Design taste at scale means consistency without bottlenecks. The design lead doesn't need to review every artifact. The head of design doesn't need to enforce the accessibility bar manually. The critique quality bar is in the system. Picture this: a new designer joins on Monday. By Wednesday, her first screen looks like it was drawn by someone who's been in the system for two years. Not because she's a prodigy, but because the AI she's working with carries every principle, token, and persona your senior designers spent years refining.

And the leads whose calendars cleared? They invest in the deep craft work that differentiates - original interaction patterns, research programs, the thorny IA problems that have been sitting on the backlog because nobody had time. Speed unlocks time. Time unlocks depth. That's derisking design quality while raising the ceiling on what your best designers can accomplish. A 3x velocity improvement isn't just faster - it's a different kind of design org.

sections.title: Product Taste in the Wild
sections.triggerText: See how product design teams are encoding taste
sections.html:
Apple HIG / Material Guidelines

Decades of opinionated, specific interaction rules documented publicly. These aren't vague values - they're concrete judgment calls that constrain every pattern decision. The teams that ship best encode their own HIG-style doc internally.

Figma Config Talks and Design System Docs

The best product design teams publish not just visual tokens but product thinking: "progressive disclosure over feature density," "IA mirrors the user's mental model, not the database schema," "every empty state teaches the next action." These rules travel straight into AI prompts.

Custom Claude Projects with Figma Libraries

Product designers are building AI configurations loaded with their design principles, token files, persona docs, and research repositories. Visual rules, interaction rules, accessibility rules, activation metrics. The pattern is consistent: document specificity, connect it, and the cleanup tax disappears.

A design system without taste is just a component library. Taste without research is just personal preference. The product designer who deploys both - their visual craft, their product judgment, and AI as an extension of their principles - produces work that's not just consistent but opinionated. That's what makes your design thinking defensible and your position irreplaceable. Every rule you refine compounds across every future interaction. After 6 months, your AI carries judgment that would take a new designer a year to absorb. That's not a productivity hack. That's a moat.

Your design judgment is the result of thousands of reps. The eye that catches a weight mismatch across the page. The sense for information hierarchy that puts the right content at the right level. The aesthetic instinct that knows warm neutrals over cool grays, 80px section padding, high-contrast CTAs over outlines. The craft that separates work that looks designed from work that looks generated.

Here's the thing. You ask AI for a design concept and get something structurally adequate, aesthetically generic. All your opinions about typography, spacing, color, interaction design - invisible. You're spending 30 minutes per component translating generic output into your reality. Do the math: 30 minutes times every component, every page, every iteration - you're burning hours a week just making AI output not embarrassing. That's a tax on undocumented taste.

Imagine if every AI-generated component already used your tokens, your spacing scale, your type pairings. The translation step from generic patterns to your reality - gone. Picture this: starting a new feature feels like picking up where your design system left off, not explaining it from scratch. What's stopping you from encoding your eye this week?

sections.title: Write Your Design Taste Doc
sections.triggerText: See how to encode your visual and UX judgment
sections.html:
Be Brutally Specific

"Body text never exceeds 18px. Primary CTAs use high-contrast fills, never outlines. Section padding is 80px minimum on desktop. Warm neutrals over cool grays. Progressive disclosure over feature density. Information architecture mirrors the user's mental model, not the database schema."

Cover All Dimensions

One product designer wrote 40 specific principles spanning persona-driven content hierarchy, UX flow logic, progressive disclosure patterns, and visual taste. AI concepts matched her thinking and aesthetic without a single correction. First try.

Include Your System

Document your component structure, design tokens, and accessibility requirements alongside your aesthetic rules. When AI generates code, it should use your actual system with your actual constraints.

Deploy It

Add to a Claude Project or CLAUDE.md. Connect your component library docs via MCP. Test by asking for a component - if it uses your tokens, your spacing, your aesthetic, you're there.

A design engineer connected his component library, tokens, and accessibility requirements. Generated code uses his actual components. The translation step from generic patterns to his reality - gone. The gap between Figma and production closed. Zero rework cycles, zero "that's not how we do it here" feedback, zero fights about token values.

Think about it like this. A chess grandmaster sees boards in patterns nobody taught her. A design engineer who's built 200 interfaces has the same compressed judgment - but only in her head. Write it down and you give your AI the same eye you spent years developing. That's not just faster output. It's freedom to focus on the deep craft - information architecture decisions, interaction nuance, the hard UX problems that differentiate your product from everything that looks the same. The time you reclaim goes to the work that actually pushes the aesthetic forward, not the work that just maintains it.

sections.title: Build a Design Taste System for Your Team
sections.triggerText: Learn how to scale design quality systematically
sections.html:
The Consistency Problem

When the team's AI draws from documented design principles - visual standards, UX patterns, accessibility requirements, component conventions - every output starts from the same baseline. The "that's not how it was designed" QA cycle disappears.

Three Layers

System taste: Design tokens, component standards, accessibility rules. Non-negotiable foundations.
Pattern taste: How you approach information architecture, interaction design, progressive disclosure. The UX philosophy.
Craft taste: Individual aesthetic refinements. The designer who has strong opinions about type pairing. The alpha layer.

The Payoff

New designers' AI-generated concepts carry the team's visual identity from day one. Senior designers reclaim time from pattern-policing and invest it in the creative exploration that actually pushes the product forward.

Every design principle you refine, every UX pattern you document, every accessibility rule you encode - these compound across every future interaction. Your taste doc becomes a living asset. After 6 months, your AI carries design judgment a new hire would need a year to develop. After 12 months, your entire product has a visual coherence your competitors can't touch because they're still fighting the same battles you automated.

Picture this. A new designer joins and her first prototype already uses your exact tokens, your exact spacing scale, your exact opinions about interaction timing. Not because she memorized the system - because the AI carrying her work was trained on it. That's onboarding compressed from months to days, and quality compounded across every pixel your team ships.

sections.title: Design Taste Tools in Production
sections.triggerText: See the tools design engineers are deploying
sections.html:
Airbnb

Design principles so specific that AI tools reference them directly. The specificity is why AI-generated design explorations feel like Airbnb - not like a template.

Figma Config Insights

Top teams encode product thinking alongside visual style - not just how it looks, but how information flows, how interactions progress, how complexity is managed.

Vercel's v0

Teams that connect their design system tokens and component libraries get output that ships. Teams without context get generic React. Same model, different economics.

Custom Claude Projects

Brand designers with 40 visual principles. UX designers with interaction frameworks. The pattern: document specificity, connect it, and the 30-minute translation tax disappears.

Taste documentation turns a design system from a reference library into an opinionated creative engine. Knowledge provides the components. Taste provides the judgment about how to use them. Together they produce design output that's not just systematic but beautiful - in the way that makes your craft defensible and your eye irreplaceable. You're not outsourcing your aesthetic. You're compounding it through agents that build with you. That's the whole game for anyone who takes craft seriously.

When you're wearing every hat, your taste IS the product. Your strategic judgment, your design eye, your engineering standards, your brand voice - it all lives in one place: your head. And every time you prompt AI, it starts from scratch. The rewrite tax hits you across every domain, every day. Do the math: 45 minutes rewriting a brief, 30 minutes fixing a component, 20 minutes rewriting landing copy, 15 minutes cleaning up an email. That's 2+ hours a day lost to translating generic output into YOUR reality.

Here's the thing. A solo founder described the fix as having a business partner with perfect memory and zero ego. That's the framing. You're not creating documentation - you're building yourself a collaborator who carries your standards across everything you touch. A collaborator who doesn't sleep, doesn't forget, and doesn't need onboarding. What's the ROI on hiring a senior operator who works for free? Infinite.

Imagine if every domain you work in already knew your rules. Your landing pages start from your voice. Your code uses your stack opinions. Your emails sound like you wrote them. What's stopping you from building that collaborator this weekend?

sections.title: Write Your Multi-Domain Taste Doc
sections.triggerText: See how to encode taste when you're doing everything
sections.html:
Cover Every Hat

When you're solo, one taste doc covers all your domains. Write your rules for each: product decisions, design aesthetic, engineering standards, brand voice, pricing philosophy. 5-10 rules per domain, one document.

The 20-Minute Sprint

For each domain, answer: "What would I tell a brilliant contractor on their first day?" Be specific - not "make it look good" but "warm neutrals, generous spacing, body text at 16px, section padding at 64px minimum."

Deploy Everywhere

Upload to a Claude Project for general work. Add a CLAUDE.md to your repo for code. Connect Notion via MCP for ongoing context. One investment, every tool carries your taste.

The 45-minute rewrite tax, multiplied across every domain you touch, is catastrophic for a solo operator. Kill it once and you reclaim hours every week. Those hours are the difference between grinding on fixing AI output and shipping the next feature, running the next customer call, building the next thing that matters.

Think about it like this. A poker player who wrote down her tells doesn't lose to players who can read them. A solo founder who wrote down her taste doesn't lose to teams 10x her size - because her AI plays her hand at the level she plays it. Put a dollar value on those 2 reclaimed hours a day. At even $100/hour blended rate, that's $50k of operational leverage per year for a 90-minute writing session. This is the single highest-return investment a solo operator can make. Nothing else comes close.

sections.title: Make Taste Compound Across All Your Work
sections.triggerText: Learn how to build a compounding taste system solo
sections.html:
The Living Document

Your taste doc isn't static. Every time you correct AI output, add the rule. "Headlines always lead with the outcome, not the feature." "API error responses include the fix, not just the error." "Landing pages start with the pain, not the solution." The doc grows smarter with every project.

Examples as Leverage

Upload your best work alongside the rules. Your best landing page, your cleanest code, your most effective email - the AI learns from your actual output, not just your rules about output.

The Compound Effect

After a few months, your AI carries judgment across every domain you work in. Starting a new project feels like having a partner who already knows your standards, your preferences, your quality bar. That's leverage no amount of hustle can replicate.

Knowledge without taste is research you never act on. Taste without knowledge is guessing at scale. As a solo operator, you need both working together - your ongoing judgment, your accumulated standards, and AI extending your capacity. That combination creates output that's unmistakably yours across everything you build.

Picture this. Six months from now, you start a new project. Your AI already knows your stack, your voice, your aesthetic, your pricing philosophy, your landing page structure. What used to take you a week to spin up takes a day. That's the compound effect for solo operators: every project makes the next one faster, sharper, more unmistakably yours. That's the whole game when you're a team of one.

Your engineering taste - architecture review criteria, "too clever" detectors, build-vs-buy judgment, the opinion about when to optimize versus ship - took years of real decisions to build. It lives in your head and gets deployed through code reviews. Which means every PR is an expensive teaching moment instead of a quality gate. Every outage is tuition on patterns you already paid for once.

Here's the thing. The AI your team uses has zero access to your engineering judgment. It generates code that's technically correct and culturally wrong. Your senior engineers spend their time catching violations of patterns the author didn't know existed. Do the math: 3 senior engineers burning 20% of their week on pattern-policing is $150k+ of wasted senior capacity per year. That's an engineer you're not hiring because you're using the one you have to enforce standards that should be in the system.

Imagine if every PR came in already following your conventions. Your senior engineers stop policing patterns and start discussing architecture tradeoffs. Picture this: code review becomes the high-leverage design conversation it was supposed to be, not the "please follow our style guide" tax it became. What's stopping you from encoding your engineering brain this sprint?

sections.title: Encode Your Engineering Taste
sections.triggerText: See how to document engineering judgment
sections.html:
Your Code Taste Doc

Write the rules that define quality on your team: "Services are named by what they do, not what they contain. API responses always include pagination metadata. Error messages tell the user what to do next, not what went wrong."

Architecture Review Criteria

Document what makes a design "too clever," when to optimize versus ship, and how to evaluate build-vs-buy decisions. This is the judgment that currently only exists in senior engineers' heads.

Anti-Patterns and Scars

The things that broke production. The patterns that looked elegant but didn't scale. Encode these alongside the taste rules - they prevent your AI from reopening every healed wound.

Deploy via CLAUDE.md

Commit to the repo. Every engineer and every AI interaction respects your engineering taste from the next git pull.

One engineering lead encoded his architecture review criteria. Junior engineers' AI-assisted proposals now match his quality bar. That's underwriting the quality of every technical decision across the team - not through review bottlenecks, but through encoded taste that scales.

The shift: code review moves from "please follow our patterns" to "let's discuss the architectural tradeoff here." Defense to offense. That's a different engineering culture. Think about it like this: a chess coach who writes down her opening principles doesn't have to explain them in every match - her students play them from move one. Your taste doc does the same thing for your engineering team. The juniors don't need to absorb your judgment through a hundred painful PR comments. They get it from day one, in every line the AI helps them write.

sections.title: Build Engineering Taste Infrastructure
sections.triggerText: Learn how to scale engineering judgment across your team
sections.html:
The Taste Stack

Platform taste: Architecture principles, deployment philosophy, reliability standards. The non-negotiable layer.
Team taste: How you approach state management, testing, API design. The execution standards.
Individual taste: The engineer who's the expert on performance. The one with the best API design instincts. The alpha layer.

Share Across Functions

Connect engineering taste docs to Notion via MCP. When PMs spec features, the AI surfaces your constraints automatically. The "we can't do it that way" feedback loop compresses from days to the moment the spec is written.

The Quality Multiplier

New engineers produce code matching senior standards on the first draft. Onboarding drops from months to weeks. Code review shifts to architecture discussions and design tradeoffs - the high-leverage work your team never had time for.

The time your senior engineers reclaim from pattern-policing goes to architecture decisions, system design, and the hardest technical problems in the backlog. That's not just better output - it's a fundamentally better use of your most expensive, most capable people.

Picture this. Traditional engineering onboarding: 3-6 months before a new hire is writing code that doesn't need reshaping. With encoded taste: day one, they're producing code that matches your conventions because the AI is enforcing them upstream. Annualized cost of that ramp compression: $80-150k per hire in recovered senior time plus reduced quality drift. Run this across 10 engineers and you're talking about a million-dollar cost structure change - from a one-time writing investment. The firms that do this first will operate on engineering economics their competitors literally cannot match.

sections.title: Engineering Taste in the Wild
sections.triggerText: See how engineering teams encode taste
sections.html:
Stripe

10+ years of opinions about naming, versioning, error handling, and backwards compatibility - encoded so new engineers' AI-generated code follows Stripe's API philosophy from day one.

Shopify

Discovered the AI bottleneck wasn't the model - it was the context. Their internal Sidekick system feeds codebase-specific patterns and conventions into every interaction.

.cursorrules and CLAUDE.md

Thousands of teams ship these context files encoding conventions, preferences, and constraints. One file per repo. Immediate acceptance rate jump. The pattern has spread across open-source because the ROI is obvious.

Engineering taste is the difference between code that compiles and code that belongs. Between a team that generates code and one that ships with conviction. The leader who encodes their engineering judgment doesn't just improve output quality - they change what their team spends time on. And that change compounds with every sprint. After 6 months, your AI carries architectural judgment a new staff engineer would need years to absorb. That's not a productivity hack. That's a moat your competitors can't buy even if they're running the same models.

Your creative judgment is the thing clients are actually paying for. The voice instinct that knows when messaging is on-brand versus just "close enough." The visual sense that catches what's wrong before you can explain it. The campaign instinct that separates a concept that resonates from one that's technically fine. The taste that took a decade of reps to build and still makes you the last human in the chain on every deliverable.

Here's the thing. Your AI has zero access to any of it. Your brand guidelines say "be clear and professional" - a nothing-statement that produces nothing work. Your team can't ship without your review because the only quality gate is your calendar. You become the bottleneck on every piece of creative output. Do the math: 30 minutes per review, 40 reviews a week, you're losing 20 hours of creative leadership time to work that should have been right the first time.

Imagine if every concept your team generated already carried your brand voice, your visual rules, your campaign philosophy. The review cycle compresses from days to minutes. Picture this: your calendar finally clear for the deep brand work - the vision, the creative leaps, the strategy that no amount of review meetings can produce. What's stopping you from encoding your creative eye this week?

sections.title: Encode Your Creative Taste
sections.triggerText: See how to build a creative taste document
sections.html:
Beyond Brand Guidelines

Not "be conversational" - that's useless. Instead: "Headlines lead with the emotional truth, not the feature. We use sentence case, never title case. Humor is dry and observational, never slapstick. We acknowledge the reader's intelligence - no explaining the obvious."

Visual Taste Rules

"Illustrations use a warm, hand-drawn quality. Photography shows real spaces with natural light. Color palette is warm neutrals with one saturated accent. Whitespace is a feature, never a gap to fill."

Campaign Philosophy

Document how you evaluate creative concepts, what makes a campaign brief strong, and how you think about audience tension. This is the judgment that makes your creative direction valuable.

Examples as Anchors

Upload your best campaigns, on-brand copy, and visual direction decks to a Claude Project. The AI learns your taste from examples of what "right" looks like.

Duolingo encoded their character voice so precisely that AI-generated content matches the tone their team took years to develop. New writers produce on-brand drafts from day one because the taste is in the system. That's the model: encode specificity and the quality scales.

Think about it like this. A creative director at a consumer brand agency wrote down the 20 specific rules she uses to evaluate a concept: whether it earns attention in 3 seconds, whether the tension is visible without a caption, whether it feels inevitable or forced. Her AI now generates concept directions that land inside her taste zone on the first pass. Her team's concept-to-approval cycle compressed from 9 days to 2. But here's the more interesting outcome - clients started asking how her agency was producing work this consistent across 6 different creatives. Her agency's win rate on new business pitches jumped. The taste doc turned into a competitive moat the competitors couldn't see and couldn't replicate.

Your calendar clears. The time goes to the work that actually matters - brand evolution, creative strategy, the concepts that differentiate the company.

sections.title: Scale Creative Quality Across Your Team
sections.triggerText: Learn how to remove the creative bottleneck
sections.html:
The Three Layers

Brand taste: Voice, visual identity, creative principles that define the brand. Non-negotiable.
Channel taste: How the social team writes versus how the content team writes. How campaign creative differs from evergreen. The format-level execution.
Craft taste: Individual creative strengths. The copywriter who nails headlines. The designer with type mastery. Personal excellence that layers on top.

Deploy It

Notion database of creative taste docs, connected via MCP. Every team member's AI draws from the full creative stack. A new writer's first draft carries the brand voice. A new designer's first concept carries the visual identity.

The Unlock

Taste scales without bottlenecks. You stop being the reviewer of everything and start being the creative leader who has time for the deep brand work - the thinking, the vision, the creative leaps that no amount of process can produce.

When you encode only visual taste, you get consistent design and inconsistent everything else. When you encode voice, visual identity, campaign philosophy, and creative strategy - every output carries the organization's creative judgment. That's the difference between a team that uses AI and one that produces genuinely branded work at scale.

Picture this. A new writer joins on Monday. By Wednesday, her first draft sounds like it was written by someone who's absorbed your brand voice for three years. Not because she's a prodigy - because the AI carrying her work was trained on your taste doc. That's creative onboarding teleported from months to days. And the senior creatives whose calendars just opened up? They're not filling them with more reviews. They're investing in the brand evolution work that only they can do.

sections.title: Creative Taste Tools
sections.triggerText: See the tools creative teams are deploying
sections.html:
Airbnb's Design Principles

So specific that AI tools reference them directly. The result: AI-generated design explorations feel like Airbnb, not like templates.

Notion's Writing Guide

Specifies voice characteristics, sentence structures to avoid, and content framing by type. Why Notion's communications feel consistent across hundreds of team members.

Custom Claude Projects

Brand designers with 40 visual principles. Copywriters with voice frameworks. Campaign strategists with positioning rules. Document specificity, connect it, and the rewrite tax disappears.

Taste documentation turns a brand guide from a shelf ornament into a creative engine. Knowledge provides the context. Taste provides the creative judgment. Together they produce work that's not just on-brand but alive - in the way that makes your creative direction defensible and your eye irreplaceable. You're not outsourcing your creative instincts. You're compounding them through a system that carries your judgment into every piece of work your team ships. That's the whole game for anyone whose creative eye is the product.

You know the feeling. You have a strong aesthetic - the way you like your code structured, the visual style you're drawn to, the tools and patterns that feel right. But every time you prompt AI, it gives you textbook output. Generic React. Default styles. Someone else's conventions. The vibe is gone. The thing that made you want to build in the first place - your specific taste - nowhere in the output.

Here's the thing. Your taste is the thing that makes your projects yours. The framework opinions, the design sensibility, the specific way you like things built. It took dozens of side projects to develop. And right now, your AI can't see any of it. Every Saturday session starts with 30 minutes of fixing AI output just to match your style. That's the difference between a productive weekend and an abandoned repo.

Imagine if every component the AI generated already used your exact spacing scale, your type pairings, your favorite libraries, your naming conventions. No translation step. Just YOUR code. Picture this: you have an idea on a Friday night, and by Saturday lunch you've shipped something that looks like you built it from scratch over a month. What's stopping you from giving your AI your vibe this weekend?

sections.title: Write Your Vibe Doc
sections.triggerText: See how to encode your personal coding aesthetic
sections.html:
Your Aesthetic Rules

Not best practices from a blog - YOUR practices from building things: "Warm neutrals over cool grays. Generous spacing. No border-radius above 12px. Body text at 16px Inter. Section padding at least 64px."

Your Stack Opinions

"Always use server components for data fetching. Tailwind for styling, never CSS-in-JS. Prefer Zustand over Redux for anything under 50 components. Framer Motion for transitions, GSAP only for scroll-triggered animations."

Your Anti-Patterns

"Never use Redux for a project this size. No utility-first class names longer than 6 values without extraction. No magic numbers in layout code."

Deploy It

Add to CLAUDE.md or a Claude Project. The next time you ask for a component, it should feel like your code - not a tutorial example.

The 30 minutes of fixing AI output to match your reality is what kills momentum on a Saturday project. Do the math: 30 minutes lost on every component, 5-10 components a day, you're burning 2-5 hours per weekend session just to translate generic output into YOUR code. That's the weekend. That's why the repo dies.

Think about it like this. A chess player with 10,000 games has pattern recognition nobody taught her. A vibe coder with 50 side projects has the same compressed judgment - but only in her head. Write it down and your AI plays your hand at the level you play it. When your AI actually knows your style, you go from idea to YOUR code without the translation step. That's the difference between a productive weekend and an abandoned repo. That's the difference between someone who ships and someone who just tinkers.

sections.title: Make Your Taste Compound Across Projects
sections.triggerText: Learn how to build a personal taste system that grows
sections.html:
The Living Taste Doc

Every time you correct AI output, add the rule. Every time you discover a pattern you love, document it. Every time something breaks your aesthetic, note the anti-pattern. The doc grows smarter with every project you ship.

Examples Over Rules

Upload your best work to a Claude Project. Your cleanest component, your best landing page, your most satisfying interaction. The AI learns your vibe from examples - sometimes better than from explicit rules.

Cross-Project Context

Use Cursor's .cursorrules per project for stack-specific context. Use Claude Projects for persistent personal taste that carries across everything. After 5 projects, starting a new one feels like picking up where you left off.

Every project you build refines your taste doc. After a few months, your AI carries your entire aesthetic and technical sensibility. You become the builder whose side projects have a recognizable style - not because you spend hours polishing, but because your taste is in the system.

Picture this. Six months from now, you look back at your GitHub and see 8 projects you shipped that all feel like you made them. Not because you grinded - because every project made the next one faster, sharper, more unmistakably yours. That's the compound effect for creative coders: the vibe accumulates, the speed increases, and the work starts looking like an artist's catalog instead of a bunch of tutorial clones. That's the whole game when you build for love.
